text: Video demonstrating the performance of a robot assisted gastric bypass. The patient's a forty five year old male who had a BMI of forty eight with gastroesophageal reflux disease and hypertension. Patient had a small hiatal hernia which was diagnosed on endoscopy.
The surgeon dissects the hiatus and the angle of His, separating the esophagus from the right and left crura. The esophagus is encircled to achieve adequate length, followed by closure of the hiatus.

In patients with obesity, especially when constructing a gastric bypass, repairing a hiatal hernia may help with creation of a small gastric pouch. In this case, probably the pouch could have been created even without dissecting the hiatus. However, since the endoscopy showed a hiatal hernia, we decided to go ahead and repair it. Our practice is to repair it when it's possible.
The surgeon places three 0 Ethibond pledgeted sutures to complete the hiatal repair, with the large liver size limiting access during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a perigastric approach to define a retrogastric plane and create a small gastric pouch approximately five centimeters from the gastroesophageal junction. A stapler with reinforcement is employed to form the gastric pouch.
The surgeon inserts a 34-French Ewald tube to calibrate the gastric pouch, followed by vertical stapling along the lesser curve to complete the creation of the small gastric pouch.
The surgeon completes the gastric pouch and proceeds to divide the greater omentum, preparing the surgical field for the gastroenterostomy procedure.
The surgeon divides the greater omentum to facilitate the gastroenterogenostomy, ensuring minimal tension during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz by reflecting the transverse mesocolon. A 50-centimeter biliary limb is constructed without performing an omental division or using a vessel sealer.
The surgeon performs a gastrotomy and jejunotomy, creating a larger biliopancreatic limb tailored for diabetic patients.

The surgeon uses a blue load stapler to create the gastrojejunal anastomosis, calibrated at approximately thirty millimeters. Following this, the Ewald tube is advanced, and a two OV absorbable V-lock suture is employed to close the common enterotomy in two layers, running the suture backwards to complete the anastomosis.
We used to perform this anastomosis with two ovicle suture. However, the V-lock has proven to be much more efficient, especially with the robotic system.

The surgeon converts the loop gastrojejunostomy to a Roux-en-Y configuration by dividing the afferent limb. A white load robotic stapler is used to create a biliopancreatic limb on the patient's left side and a Roux limb on the right side.

The surgeon measures approximately one hundred centimeters distal on the Roux limb and performs a side-to-side anastomosis using the H-shaped technique. The stapler is fired bidirectionally to create the H-shaped anastomosis, with the first staple line in one direction. The common enterotomy is closed transversely using a stapler after the second staple line is fired.
The surgeon closes the mesentery defect using a permanent 2-0 Ethibond suture, ensuring careful placement to avoid creating a mesenteric hematoma.

The surgeon exposes Peterson's defect by reflecting the Roux limb to the patient's left side and lifting the transverse mesocolon. The defect is then closed by suturing it to the edge of the transverse colon.
The surgeon performs an endoscopy to visually inspect the anastomosis, ensuring the integrity and proper formation of the surgical connection.
